title: Confucius (Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy)
excerpts:
Section Title: Confucius > 5. The Family and the State
Content:
When it comes to the mandate inherited from King Wen, the chapter
insists that the mandate is not unchanging, and so as ruler the son
must always be mindful of it when deciding how to act. Further, it is
not always possible to understand Heaven, but the “feelings of
the people are visible”, and so the ruler must care for his
subjects. The Zhou political view that Confucius inherited was based
on supernatural intercession to place a person with personal virtue in
charge of the state, but over time the emphasis shifted to the way
that the effects of good government could be viewed as proof of a
continuing moral justification for that placement.

title: The Analects, Book 3, by Confucius
excerpts:
Section Title: The Analects > translated by James Legge (1893) > Book 3: Pa Yih > Chapter 1
Content:
Confucius said of the head of the Chi family, who had eight rows of pantomimes in his area, 'If he can bear to do this, what may he not bear to do?'
 ... 
Section Title: The Analects > translated by James Legge (1893) > Book 3: Pa Yih > Chapter 11
Content:
Someone asked the meaning of the great sacrifice. The Master said, 'I do not know. He who knew its meaning would find it as easy to govern the kingdom as to look on this' — pointing to his palm.
 ... 
Section Title: The Analects > translated by James Legge (1893) > Book 3: Pa Yih > Chapter 18
Content:
The Master said, 'The full observance of the rules of propriety in serving one's prince is accounted by people to be flattery.'

title: Confucius - Simple English Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
excerpts:
Section Title: Contents > Teaching
Content:
These were traditional relationships called the 'five prototypes'.
Confucius said that in all these relationships, both people must obey rules.
For example, a subject must obey a prince, but also a prince must listen to a subject and must rule him well and fairly.
Confucius said that people should only do things to other people if they would be okay with other people doing those things to themselves.
This is sometimes called the Golden Rule and was also taught by Jesus Christ .
His students wrote down small stories about him, and things that he said.
These were put together to make a book called The Analects , which became one of the main books of Confucianism .

title: Analects of Confucius 論語 - Charles Muller
excerpts:
[20-3] 子曰。不知命、無以爲君子也。不知禮、無以立也。不知言、無以知人也。
[20:3] The Master said: “If you do not understand destiny, there is no way you will be regarded as a Noble Man. If you do not understand propriety, there is no way for you to be established. If you do not understand words, there is no way for you to understand people.”
Notes
1. Shijing . The Book of Odes . One of the five Chinese classics 五經 ; also commonly referred to in Chinese as the Mao shi 毛詩 . Also translated as the Book of Poetry , or Book of Songs . It is a collection of poems, written during the 500 years between the beginning of the Zhou dynasty and the middle of the Spring and Autumn period. It is believed that Confucius selected 305 from more than 3,000 pieces and edited them into a book to be used for education. Of the 305 poems, each is usually known by its title, which are drawn from phrases found in its opening passage.
2.This simile for the process of self-perfection is found often in Confucian texts.

title: The Analects :: Book 3
excerpts:
Section Title: n o thingistic. o rg > ... > Chapter I. > Confucius's indignation at the usurpation of royal rites.
Content:
Confucius said of the head of the Chî family, who had eight
rows of pantomimes in his area, "If he can bear to do this, what
may he not bear to do?"
 ... 
Section Title: n o thingistic. o rg > The Analects > Chapter 11. > The profound meaning of the great sacrifice.
Content:
Some one asked the meaning of the great sacrifice. The Master
said, "I do not know. He who knew its meaning would find it as easy to govern the kingdom as to look on this" -- pointing to his palm.
